slides.title: Functions in Disguise 🥸
slides.content:
## Re-thinking Functions

Usually, we declare a function using the `fun` keyword, give it a Name, and then call it from somewhere else.

But what if we just need a tiny, single-use piece of logic? Writing out an entire named `fun` block is exhausting. Enter: The **Lambda**.

slides.title: The Lambda Syntax { }
slides.content:
A Lambda is just a tiny, anonymous function surrounded entirely by curly braces `{}`.

You can even store this block of logic inside a standard variable!

quizData.question: What happens if you define `val laugh = { println("Haha") }` but never actually write `laugh()` anywhere?

options.text: Nothing prints. The lambda logic was stored, but never executed.

quizData.explanation: A Lambda is still just a recipe (a function). Assigning it to a variable merely stores the instructions. You must still actively "call" it `()` to execute the logic.

slides.title: Lambda Parameters ⬅️
slides.content:
Lambdas can accept parameters, too! 

You place the variable names at the very start of the curly braces, followed by a right-facing arrow `->`. The executable logic comes after the arrow.

quizData.question: What symbol separates the incoming parameters from the actual executable logic inside a Lambda `{}`?
quizData.explanation: The `->` visually splits the Lambda in half: Input Parameters on the left, Executable Logic on the right.
